#### 4.3.3 Adequacy and Use of Performance Measurement Data
The FI has successfully implemented an internal performance measurement strategy and performance measurement tools that are used by all partners. To facilitate roll-up of performance data and maximize its use, the PMS and associated tools could be streamlined to identify critical information needed.
Reporting on Performance Outcomes
Each FI responsibility centre collects performance data annually and provides it to PHAC through the performance measurement tool (PMT). PHAC then has the responsibility to roll up all the data to provide information that will monitor overall performance and inform decision making.
In 2013-14 and 2014-15, performance measurement data collected by responsibility centres was rolled up by PHAC, who then provided partners with an internal FI annual report. Internal key informants indicated that these reports were very informative and useful, as they allowed partners to reflect on their activities and achievements each year. However, an annual report has not been produced since 2014-15, even though responsibility centers have continued to provide their data to PHAC. A lack of capacity and resources were cited as reasons why reports have not been produced in recent years. Still, PHAC has managed to fulfill its reporting obligation on the FI, as overall performance information has been published on an annual basis through PHAC's Departmental Results Report.
The evaluation found that, while detailed performance data is being collected, the process could be made more efficient by streamlining several performance indicators and dropping others where validity appears low. In addition, limits on data availability in some areas (i.e., First Nations on-reserve communities) make it difficult to complete the current PMT. There is a need to review performance indicators in the PMS to ensure that they are in line with data that is available to be requested, as per the current contribution agreements with Indigenous communities.
Supporting a Performance Culture - Grants and Contributions
Over the last two years, PHAC has been working to enhance the culture of performance measurement within community-based organizations funded by PHAC. As such, the CAF introduced a requirement for recipients to report on the short- and medium-term outcomes of their programs. To help support funding recipients in developing this capacity, recipients are able to use up to 10% of their funding to that end. PHAC has also provided training and other resources to guide recipients in developing their evaluation and performance measurement capacity. Footnote 55 Footnote 56 Footnote 57
Such a culture change can be difficult to implement, as benefits associated with proper use of performance measurement can take years to appear. Still, early data collected from external key informants has already shown how performance information can positively influence the design and delivery of community-based programs. As such, one non-governmental organization reported that the work they did elaborating the program theory of their intervention allowed them to identify gaps in their intervention in terms of supporting behavioural changes, which they addressed by modifying the design of their intervention.
The current approach for collecting performance data and evaluation results from community-based projects will allow PHAC to better understand what interventions are working well, thus helping organizations make decisions about where projects could be scaled up or adapted in other communities. Several external key informants were also interested in learning more about what other interventions were working well and what could potentially be applied to their communities.
There is a continued need for federal investments and activities, as HIV/AIDS remains a persistent public health issue that disproportionately affects priority populations. Prevention, diagnosis, care, and treatment are essential to manage and control HIV and other related sexually transmitted and blood-borne infections (STBBI). The estimated HIV incidence rate in Canada has shown a slight increase since 2014; additional years of data are needed to determine whether this represents a true increase because of the wide plausible ranges around these estimates. Rates of related STBBI are also on the rise.
Success in HIV treatment has resulted in a worldwide momentum to eliminate new infections. To this end, Canada has endorsed the United Nations Program on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS) and the World Health Organization's (WHO) global health sector strategies to address HIV, viral hepatitis, and sexually transmitted infections (STI) with the goal of eliminating STBBI as a health concern by 2030.
The FI activities align well with the legislated mandate and responsibilities of each of its federal partners. Partners and external stakeholders understand FI roles and responsibilities well. External stakeholders see the Government of Canada as having an important role in national and international coordination, as well as in knowledge translation for HIV/STBBI prevention, care, treatment, support, and research.
FI activities conducted by all four partners have contributed to increased awareness and better practices to prevent the acquisition and transmission of HIV for both priority populations and target audiences. Priority populations were provided with direct interventions (e.g., in correctional institutions, on reserves) or community-based activities that increased their knowledge and awareness. Target audiences were also provided with webinars, guidelines, and other knowledge products that increased their knowledge and awareness.
Findings indicate that while awareness and use of knowledge products developed by PHAC on HIV- and other STBBI is high among practitioners working in the sexual health field, there are still gaps among primary care providers. Thus, there are opportunities to expand the reach of FI-developed knowledge products to additional networks, so that primary care providers (i.e., family and general practitioners) would increase their awareness and use of PHAC HIV and STBBI knowledge products. FI partners should also consider stakeholder information needs in areas of shared jurisdiction between federal and provincial responsibilities (e.g., the use of Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis [PrEP], testing availability), to address gaps in knowledge products.
Through the provision of services, such as laboratory sciences and access to harm reduction materials in correctional institutions, FI activities have strengthened the capacity of target audiences and priority populations to prevent and control HIV. Community-based activities funded by the FI have also supported behaviour change among priority populations through adoption of healthier sexual and drug-use behaviours, and by providing linkages to care and treatment.
However, data collected to date on PHAC-funded community-based activities indicates that some of the most at-risk populations for HIV and other STBBI (e.g., gay, bisexual, and other men who have sex with men) were not sufficiently reached over the last five years by the activities offered by community-based projects. Since 2017, through the implementation of the HIV and Hepatitis C Community Action Fund (CAF), PHAC has taken a step to address this challenge and has funded projects that demonstrate best potential for impact. As such, CAF has refocused funding priorities, priority populations, and eligible activities. Footnote 58
In addition, PHAC has also implemented a new Harm Reduction Fund aimed at reducing HIV and hepatitis C among people who share injection and inhalation drug-use equipment, thus supporting community-based intervention with the most at-risk populations. Footnote 59

### 5.3 Economy and Efficiency
Through the Responsibility Centre Committee (RCC), the FI has put in place an oversight and governance body that fosters information exchange and a high level of collaboration between FI partners. The FI has also put mechanisms in place that allow partners to collaborate with stakeholders. They have also established useful ways to ensure consultation with stakeholders on various files.
With high levels of collaboration between federal partners and the involvement of external stakeholders, the FI was able to put together a federal response that is seen by internal and external stakeholders as generally coherent. However, some stakeholders, including provincial representatives, identified gaps in the overall response. As such, they have called for additional coordinated action to address stigma and discrimination as it relates to HIV and other STBBI, and to increase access to testing technology.
The FI authorities have allowed partners to link HIV/AIDS programs with other related infection programs to maximize the effectiveness of their activities and ensure an integrated response. Within this context, partners have worked over the last five years to increase integration of activities to address HIV with other related STBBI and improve the response. While the majority of stakeholders have been supportive of this integration, and have welcomed the new Pan-Canadian STBBI Framework for Action, they require more information on what actions FI partners will take to address disease-specific targets as part of this approach.
While the FI has successfully implemented a comprehensive internal performance measurement strategy (PMS) and FI partners have collected performance data over the years, the use of this data for decision making is limited, as a summary of the data has not been compiled in recent years. Identifying critical information would help guide a streamlining of information collected through the PMS and associated tools. This would help summarize performance data and maximize its use.
Recommendation 1 (for all FI partners)
Determine how federal investments will contribute to the goals outlined in the Pan-Canadian STBBI Framework for Action, including reducing STBBI-related stigma and discrimination, and aligning investments with those populations with the highest burden of STBBI. Communicate this to external stakeholders and Canadians.
The Pan-Canadian STBBI Framework for Action sets out an overarching and comprehensive approach to support and contribute to achieving global STBBI targets (see Appendix 4 for disease-specific targets included in the Framework). While integration of the federal HIV response with its response to STBBI is well supported, internal and external stakeholders are unsure which actions will be taken by federal partners within this framework. As such, stakeholders need more clarity on what actions the FI partners will take to achieve the disease-specific global targets. Additional information on addressing STBBI-related stigma and discrimination, as well as information on aligning investment to populations with the highest burden of STBBI is needed.
Recommendation 2 (specific to PHAC)
Explore partnerships and mechanisms to facilitate the dissemination and uptake of STBBI-related knowledge products.
Awareness and uptake of current HIV and related STBBI knowledge products by sexual health practitioners is high. Still, to increase the chances of accessing the undiagnosed, FI partners must work with other key stakeholders to achieve higher levels of awareness and uptake of HIV and related STBBI knowledge by all intended target audiences, including primary care practitioners. FI partners should also give further consideration to how they could better support uptake of knowledge products in areas of shared jurisdiction between federal and provincial responsibilities (e.g., guidance for the use of PrEP, access to testing technology), as there are gaps in these areas.
Recommendation 3 (for all FI partners)
Enhance the use of performance information by simplifying indicators within the current performance measurement strategy to allow for annual reporting of results.
The FI has successfully implemented a comprehensive internal performance measurement strategy and FI partners have been collecting performance data. While PHAC has managed to fulfill its reporting obligation on the FI through the Departmental Results Report, FI partners would like to see an internal annual report to monitor progress and facilitate decision making. Simplifying the indicators in the PMS and ensuring that each FI partner is able to collect required data would make preparing an annual report more feasible.

## Appendix 1 - Roles of Responsibility Centres
Public Health Agency of Canada Programs and Partnership Division (PPD) PPD is responsible for administering transfer payment programs through grants and contributions, developing approaches to address issues affecting priority populations, supporting partnerships to achieve greater national cohesion on HIV and related infections, and coordinating the FI. PPD also has primary responsibility for communications, public awareness, and global engagement.
Surveillance and Epidemiology Division (SED) SED is responsible for managing the national communicable disease surveillance system, as well as leading epidemiological research and providing technical epidemiological support to provincial and territorial governments, international institutions (i.e., World Health Organization, Pan-American Health Organization, European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control), and foreign government institutions (i.e., United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention).
Strategic Issues and Integrated Management Division (SIIMD) SIIMD is responsible for supporting the joint planning, reporting, monitoring, and program evaluation of the FI.
Professional Guidelines and Public Health Practice Division (PGPHPD) PGPHPD develops guidelines and public health intervention research, identifies and disseminates promising practices, and supports technical knowledge transfer and exchange, including modelling, estimations, and projections for HIV and related infections.
National HIV and Retrovirology Laboratories (NHRL) NHRL provide a comprehensive range of laboratory science services and expertise related to HIV and emerging retroviruses. The provision of national laboratory services for enhancing care, treatment, and diagnosis of HIV-infected Canadians is mandated under the FI. NHRL is comprised of the National Laboratory for HIV Genetics, the National Laboratory for HIV Reference Services, and the National Laboratory for HIV Immunology.
Regional Offices (ROs) PHAC maintains a presence in all regions of Canada through five regional offices (ROs) and the Northern Secretariat. The ROs are the focal point for all regional activities. The ROs facilitate the monitoring of grants and contributions projects funded under programs administered by PPD. ROs facilitate intersectoral collaboration on national STBBI priorities at the regional level and provide public health information to other PHAC responsibility centres to ensure national programs reflect local contexts and approaches.
Canadian Institutes of Health Research Institute of Infection and Immunity (III) The CIHR HIV/AIDS Research Initiative is strategically directed by the III, which is responsible for managing and overseeing the research components of the FI. The III helps to advance research on treatment, prevention, and a cure for HIV, as well as options for improving quality of life for people living with HIV, and research that focuses on priority populations in Canada. The CIHR Canadian HIV Trials Network supports HIV researchers and trainees through four research funding streams:
- Biomedical and clinical;
- Health services and population health;
- Community-based research; and
- CIHR Canadian HIV Trials Network.
Indigenous Services Canada (ISC) Communicable Disease Control Division The Communicable Disease Control Division (CDCD) of the First Nations and Inuit Health Branch (FNIHB) is the responsibility centre for the FI within ISC. CDCD works in collaboration with other FNIHB divisions to provide HIV prevention, education, awareness, and community capacity building, and to facilitate access to quality diagnosis, care, treatment, and support to on-reserve First Nations communities. They also support availability of primary health care services, such as HIV testing, for First Nations living on reserve in certain remote and isolated areas across Canada. In addition, FNIHB provides non-insured health benefits to eligible First Nations and Inuit persons, which fund access to treatments such as antiretroviral medication for HIV positive individuals.
Correctional Service Canada (CSC) Clinical and Public Health Services Branch CSC's Clinical and Public Health Services Branch provides essential health services to inmates in federal correctional institutions, including services related to the prevention, care, and treatment of HIV and associated STBBI. This includes health promotion and education, screening and testing, treatment and support for incarcerated persons who are HIV-positive or diagnosed with other STBBI, as well as discharge planning and surveillance within the institution.
This logic model demonstrates the relationship between the inputs, activities, outputs and outcomes of the Federal Initiative to Address HIV/AIDS in Canada as they relates to the delivery of the program and its governance.
The inputs for the Federal Initiative include: human resources, financial resources (operations & maintenance, grants & contributions, and awards); policies and legislative authorities; laboratory infrastructure; information technologies; Provincial-Territorial surveillance, government of Canada global agreements, corporate support and internal services, and Provincial-Territorial agreements (memoranda of understanding and memoranda of agreement).
Using these inputs, the delivery component of the Federal Initiative program conducts activities including knowledge creation; knowledge translation, synthesis and exchange; policy funding program development, and public health interventions.
Knowledge creation produces outputs of:
- national surveillance/epidemiological data collection, processes, and studies;
- new diagnostic tools and techniques;
- program assessment results;
- intervention research;
- models and analytical tools; and,
- data from funded research and clinical trials.
Knowledge translation, synthesis and exchange produce outputs of:
- evidence-based promising practices;
- knowledge synthesis reports, literature reviews or other products;
- surveillance and epidemiological reports and products;
- peer reviewed articles
- public health practice guidance
- reference materials and educational tools
- knowledge exchange tools which include presentations at conferences, webinars, mail, mobile applications, and knowledge brokers.
Policy and funding program development produces outputs of:
- policy options, advice, and briefs;
- priorities for research funding opportunities and community funding programs/ co-funding agreements;
- funding opportunities; and,
Public health interventions produce outputs of:
- laboratory standardization;
- enhanced and specialized surveillance systems;
- standardized surveillance systems
- field support and public health response
- case and clinical management;
- social marketing and awareness campaigns;
- involvement of priority populations;
- educational products and training materials;
- workshops and presentations; and,
- partnerships and collaborative networks.
The immediate outcomes for delivery of the Federal Initiative are:
- increased awareness and knowledge of ways to prevent the acquisition and control the transmission of HIV and relates SRBBIs; and
- strengthened capacity (skills, competencies and attitudes) to prevent the acquisition and transmission of HIV and relates STBBIs.
The immediate outcomes are expected to lead to intermediate outcomes of:
- Improved uptake and application of knowledge in public health practice, and,
- Increased uptake of personal behaviours that prevent the acquisition and transmission of HIV and related STBBIs.
The activities of the governance component of the federal initiative are reporting, strategic planning and partner engagement through the responsibility centres.
The outputs of these activities are parliamentary reports, horizontal reporting, strategic plans, work plans, partnership and collaboration.
The immediate outcomes of the Federal Initiative governance are:
- robust horizontal governance and coordination among partners; and,
- enhanced engagement to improve the federal response.
The immediate outcomes are expected to lead to intermediate outcomes of:
- increased coherence of the federal response; and,
- increased integration of HIV with related STBBIs and other relevant key activities to improve the response.
The ultimate outcome of both the governance and delivery components of the Federal Initiative is decreased acquisition and transmission of new infections. This is aligned with strategic outcomes of the four federal partners:
- PHAC: protecting Canadians and empowering them to improve their health
- CIHR: Canada is a world leader in the creation, dissemination and application of health research knowledge.
- ISC: First Nations communities and individuals receive health services and benefits that are responsive to their needs so as to improve their health status.
- CSC: The custody, correctional interventions, and supervision of offenders in communities and in institutions contribute to public safety.
The logic model specifies two groups of stakeholders for the Federal Initiative. These two groups are:
- "Priority populations," which include: gay men and other men who have sex with men, people who use drugs, Indigenous people, ethno-cultural communities (immigrants, migrants, and refugees), people involved in the sale, trade or purchase of sex, people living in or recently released from correctional facilities, transgender people, people living with HIV/AIDS or hepatitis C, and women and youth within those groups.
- "Audiences," which include: Provincial/Territorial governments; First Nation health authorities; public health and health care professionals; educators; non-governmental and community based organizations; researchers, academics and trainees; laboratory networks; and global partners.

A review of academic, peer-reviewed publications and grey literature was conducted to support evaluation findings. Findings from the literature review informed questions related to the relevance of the FI, including:
- Evidence on incidence and prevalence of HIV/AIDS and assessment of rates for priority populations in Canada;
- Evidence on the evolution of the HIV/AIDS epidemic and the public health response in the past five years; and
- Evidence of duplication, complementarity, and overlap between the Government of Canada's FI activities and those that fall within the purview of provinces, territories, and other stakeholders.
Representatives from the four federal departments who deliver the FI provided a series of internal documents to inform findings related to relevance, performance, and efficiency and economy of the program. These documents included annual reports, internal performance measurement data, communications products, terms of reference and meeting minutes for the various committees involved in implementing the FI, and surveillance reports.
The document review also included analysis of performance data collected as part of the horizontal Performance Measurement Strategy Framework for the FI. This included some performance information obtained by grants and contributions recipients receiving funding from CIHR and PHAC.
In total, over 400 documents were reviewed in the conduct of this line of evidence.
Key Informant Interviews:
Key informant interviews were conducted to gather in-depth information related to the relevance, performance, and efficiency of the FI. Interviews were conducted in a semi-structured manner, based on a pre-determined questionnaire. Interview questions included in each questionnaire linked directly to indicators identified in the evaluation matrix. In total, 71 interviews were held with 76 key informants. Interviewees included:
- Internal staff working within the four FI partner organizations (n=31)
- External key informants, including funding recipients, community leaders, and subject matter experts recommended by program managers within the four FI partner organizations (n=31)
- Key Informants working within FI or stakeholder organizations, with special knowledge related to three case study programs (N=14)
Key informants were located across Canada, and, as such, interviews were conducted over the phone and in group settings as needed.
Evidence collected in key informant interviews was systematically analyzed using NVIVO qualitative data analysis software. Six analysts identified relevant information related to evaluation questions and, using the software, grouped information based on themes, a process called "coding". To ensure consistency, the evaluation team conducted an inter-coder reliability assessment and generated a coding guide. After coding was completed, analysts prepared a summary of themes that emerged in the interviews. Based on this information, analysts were able to draw conclusions about the strength of particular themes.
Three case studies were conducted to provide an in-depth illustration of program activities. These case studies were selected to illustrate the achievements of FI programming and lessons learned from these activities. Case studies were selected to represent the diversity of contributions from the four FI partners, as well as the diversity of target audiences for FI activities.
These case studies included:
- The Canadian HIV/AIDS Trials Network: an HIV/AIDS research partnership network funded through the CIHR as part of the FI.
- Dried-Blood Spot Testing for HIV and Hepatitis C: an initiative lead by PHAC's National Microbiology Laboratory to enhance testing for STBBI in rural and remote communities, particularly for First Nations communities.
- Peer Education Course (PEC) and Aboriginal PEC: an education program offered to people who are incarcerated in federal correctional institutions through CSC, which offers health promotion aimed at reducing the transmission and acquisition of STBBI.
Each case study included key informant interviews and a review of internal program documents, as described above.
Data was analyzed by triangulating information gathered from the different methods listed above. The use of multiple lines of evidence and triangulation was intended to increase the reliability and credibility of the evaluation findings and conclusions.
